tokens: We have you back at the LM.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: I got to get your bag...
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: I got it; I got it.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Let me get your bag off.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: I'm reading 80 on the amps, 78 on the amps...Correction, that's amp-hours.
Motor temps are 200 and 210 on the rear, 200 and 250 on the forward.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: And let me brief you here on the close-out tonight, 17.
A number of things we're going to do here that are slightly different.
We've got some stuff for you over at the ALSEP, Jack, and I'll get with you when you go over there.
Nothing we have to worry about in the meanwhile.

When we unload the Rover, we're going to take the SESC (Special Environmental Sample Container, sometimes called the "short can") out, and we're going to use that to collect the contaminated sample out behind the footpad there as per plan, and when we take the traverse gravimeter off, we're going to want to get both a grav and a bias reading, because the pallet was (unlatched and figuratively) swinging in the breeze there.
Otherwise, let's press on with the close-out, and we'll get with you as times change.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: The core tubes are going in SCB-7...I mean...Yeah, 7.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Did you get my bag already?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: We'll have one more to put in here.
I'm just going to lay this over here.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: The big one (probably the Big Bag).
Man, there's some big ones (individual rock samples) in there, too.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: We can get some of that subfloor.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Yeah, there's one in my footpan, too.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: (Near the ladder) Why don't you leave that there for a minute?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: What did you say about the
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: I see where you've got a feedwater tone coming up pretty soon, Gene.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: (Removing Jack's PLSS SCB) Bob, I already got it and I'm in Auto (means "Aux").
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: How are we fixed for samples?
Here's (SCB) 5, and it's about 1/2 to 3/4 full.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: We've got to carry the SESC up.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: ...(from SCB-)3 in there, the Rover samples.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: We probably ought to put the SESC in there, huh?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Yeah let's put the SESC someplace...
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: Where do you want the SESC, Bob?
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Let's put the SESC someplace where it's accessible to get that contamination sample.
We probably want to get it before you go off to the ALSEP, but there's no real hurry on that.
We'll see what works in best.
I'm not sure where the most convenient place for you...
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: Why don't we get it now, and then we can have this bag...
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: We can get the bag cleaned up.
We can put it in bag 5.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: There's probably not very many convenient places to put it.
That sounds like a good idea to me.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: Let me get my scoop (off of the gate).
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: You want that...(To Gene, having reached the gate) I don't have a scoop, I don't even have a rake.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: They both fell off when that thing (the gate) opened.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Here's a full core tube we can't forget.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Yeah, why don't you get that scoop (means the LRV sampler) off (the Rover), and I'll put it (the core) over here in (SCB) 4.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: That was a good time to lose it.
I'm glad we didn't lose it (laughing) any earlier.
If we were going to lose it, that couldn't have been more ideal.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: We've got two empty core tubes.
(I) feel like we took a lot of them, though.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: We confirm that (there are two empty drive tubes and) three full core tubes...
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: If we get it in, maybe (garbled)...
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: We're going to get this SESC now, and get it out of the way, Bob.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Sort of underneath where you probably had the...
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: ...solar side of the cosmic ray experiment, there.
Between the footpad and the ALSEP doors there.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: Oh, I've got about an inch to go.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: And both your feedwaters are up (to speed), 17.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: (To Jack) (Garbled) that white thing off for me?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Here, let me get...(Pause) There, you got her.
(I'll) take a couple (of pictures) over here.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Let me just go pat the radar.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Bob, I'm on frame 96, and the short can sample - contaminated sample - is documented by two stereopairs prior to that (frame).
And the "before" is the cosmic ray pictures.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: And which SCB is that going in, Jack?
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: ...remember, I want inventories of the stuff as it comes off the Rover and where you put it over there by the footpad, so we can help you keep track of it.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: (Going to the ladder with SCB-5) Okay.
We've got the big bag...ah...bag 7, bag 5, bag 4 at the footpad.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: We've also got SCB-3 with the Rover samples in it on the Rover, if you have any...yeah, you have some of those today.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: And you want a gravity reading and a bias reading, is that correct?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: (To Jack) What did you do with the...(To himself) Okay.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: You've got another big rock over here from the...
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: (At) Station 9, I got a football-size rock, and I've put it in there.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Well, we eventually lost one clamp.
Let's see what we've got left on here (on the repaired fender).
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Gene's football-sized rock looks like it might be glass coated.
And it might even have a shatter cone or two on it.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: (To Fendell) I don't know what you're focused on...
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: ...and, Jack, we're making plans here, to change the camera usage at the end of EVA here.
And we're going to let you take Commander's camera out to the ALSEP and take a few photos which people think we need.
And Gene's going to take your camera out and document the geophone, when he deploys it.
We will not deploy it for the long-term experiment, however.
And we'll bring both (cameras) back, and carry them to the ETB when we get done.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: We've got to reverse the roles of the camera here.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: While you're getting that, we've got to doff our harnesses.
Let me...Before you take this...Are you going to start loading the ETB yet or not?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: I've got the cosmic ray in the ETB.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: It's been in there all along, hasn't it?
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: Mag Foxtrot, or Franny, I guess, we changed it to.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Jack, where is the cosmic ray?
Did you put that in the ETB already?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: I don't think the 500's working anymore, anyway.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: I'm going to go get a gravimeter reading.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: That sounds like all of them to us.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: And there are two on the cameras.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: We're ready for a bias, Gene.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: (Pause) (I'm pushing) Bias; and it is flashing.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Let me take a look around.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: It's over here on the MESA.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: (Pause) Let's get rid of these tool harnesses.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: You've come loose on the right.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Need to fix it for you.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: And remember, when they come off, guys, don't get them tangled up in the hoses.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: Let me pull it off for you.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: If you stand still, we'll have Danny untangle it.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: (Pause) Let me get the other side.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: You don't have to get it around those hoses and everything?
(Pause) See if you can do it.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Works a lot better than the simulations, doesn't it?
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: They won't find the other one.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Come over here and watch me, Ed.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: (To Ed Fendell) Nod your camera if you can see me.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: That is almost easier than at the Cape.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: Jack, wait a minute before you...Hey, Bob, are we going to need those other core tubes?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: We'd like to have you leave the two core tubes and the extension handle and the hammer and I suppose the core cap dispenser here.
If we get back in time from doing all our appointed tasks at the VIP site and at the ALSEP, we'll try and drive a double core here (pause) to end things up with a bang.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: We'll play games with the extension handle, but that's all right.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Let me set them over here.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Don't need the LRV sampler any more.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: ...extension handles when that pallet came open?
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: (Responding to Bob) Yes, but I can still drive a core with the hammer if we need to.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: One (extension handle) went with the rake, and one went with the scoop.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: (Returning to the Rover without the harness) Okay, Bob.
As I read down that page (
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: I guess I'm ready to go to the (ALSEP)...
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: I want you to do something.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: (Responding to Bob) Six mags, is it?
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: (To Jack) You want these...You don't have...
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: ...Did all the FSR's (Football-Sized Rocks) get off the Rover into the big bag?
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: This is the one you need anyway.
(Pause) Why don't you see if you can grab a couple (of tourist pictures)?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: I don't know whether I can get you.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Ed, you've got your camera in the way.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Oh, just take them straight on.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: (Taking pictures toward the Rover) That's sort of...(garbled).
(Pause) Let me get a little different (pause) focus.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: I've got to go get a neutron flux probe, I guess, (out of the deep core hole).
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: That's going to be easy to pull out.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Let's see if I've got everything in here.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: (To Bob, not having heard his "That's right") You ready for me to go to the ALSEP?
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: We're ready for both of you guys, now.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: I think we're having a site handover or something.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: (No answer) Gene, do you read me?
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: ...site changeover there, and we've got you again.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: (Hearing Bob) Are you ready for me to go to the ALSEP?
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: We're ready for both of you guys, now.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: Gene, are you ready for Jack to go to the ALSEP, now?
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: (Consulting checklist to see what Bob means) Oh, let me see.
I'm ready to get on, and go to the VIP site.
(To Jack, having finally remembered the ceremony, calling) Wait a minute, Jack.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Come on back here a minute.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: ...we're trying to be subtle there, guys.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: Come on back here a minute.
Come on back here a minute.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: I didn't realize you were going out there quite so soon.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Well, I just looked to see where it is (in the checklist).
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: What did you do with that (rock)...
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: How about one of...How about this one?
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: (Long Pause) (Garbled) that one in my footpan?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: I put it in the (laughs) big bag.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Let me get it (the rock), so you won't get it too dirty.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: How about right over here against that background.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Houston, before we close out our EVA, we understand that there are young people in Houston today who have been effectively touring our country, young people from countries all over the world, respectively, touring our country.
They had the opportunity to watch the launch of Apollo 17; (and) hopefully had an opportunity to meet some of our young people in our country.
And we'd like to say first of all, welcome, and we hope you enjoyed your stay.
Second of all, I think probably one of the most significant things we can think about when we think about Apollo is that it has opened for us - "for us" being the world - a challenge of the future.
The door is now cracked, but the promise of the future lies in the young people, not just in America, but the young people all over the world learning to live and learning to work together.

In order to remind all the people of the world in so many countries throughout the world that this is what we all are striving for in the future, Jack has picked up a very significant rock, typical of what we have here in the valley of Taurus-Littrow.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: It's a rock composed of many fragments, of many sizes, and many shapes, probably from all parts of the Moon, perhaps billions of years old.

But fragments of all sizes and shapes - and even colors - that have grown together to become a cohesive rock, outlasting the nature of space, sort of living together in a very coherent, very peaceful manner.
When we return this rock or some of the others like it to Houston, we'd like to share a piece of this rock with so many of the countries throughout the world.
We hope that this will be a symbol of what our feelings are, what the feelings of the Apollo Program are, and a symbol of mankind: that we can live in peace and harmony in the future.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: (Taking the rock as Gene hands it to him) A portion of a rock will be sent to a representative agency or museum in each of the countries represented by the young people in Houston today, and we hope that they - that rock and the students themselves - will carry with them our good wishes, not only for the new year coming up but also for themselves, their countries, and all mankind in the future.
Put that in the big bag, Geno.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: We salute you, promise of the future.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: (Pause) We thank you for your sentiments and your interest.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: (Taking the rock and returning to the Rover) And now - let me bring this (TV) camera around - to commemorate not just Apollo 17's visit to the Valley of Taurus-Littrow but as an everlasting commemoration of what the real meaning of Apollo is to the world, we'd like to uncover a plaque that has been on the leg of our spacecraft that we have climbed down many times over the last 3 days.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: And I'll read what that plaque says to you.
First of all, it has a picture of the world.
One of the North America and one of South America.
The other covers the other half of the world including Africa, Asia, Europe, Australia, covers the North Pole and the South Pole.

In between these two hemispheres, we have a pictorial view of the Moon, a pictorial view of where all the Apollo landings have been made; so that when this plaque is seen again by others who come, they will know where it all started.
The words are, "Here man completed his first exploration of the Moon, December 1972 A.D.
May the spirit of peace in which we came be reflected in the lives of all mankind." It's signed, "Eugene A.
Schmitt, and most prominently, Richard M.

Nixon, President of the United States of America." This is our commemoration that will be here until someone like us, until some of you who are out there, who are the promise of the future, come back to read it again and to further the exploration and the meaning of Apollo.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: We in Houston copy that and echo your sentiments.
(James) Fletcher (the NASA Administrator) is here beside me.
He'd like to say a word to the two of you.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: Gene and Jack, I've been in close touch with the White House, and the President has been following very closely your absolutely fascinating work up there.
He'd like to wish you Godspeed as you return to Earth, and I'd like to personally second that.
We'll see you in a few days.
transcript.speaker: Fletcher

tokens: We appreciate your comments, and we certainly appreciate those of the President.
And whether it be civilian or military, I think Jack and I would both like to give our salute to America.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Fletcher, if I may, I'd like to remind everybody, I'm sure, of something they're aware, but this valley of history has seen mankind complete its first evolutionary steps into the universe: leaving the planet Earth and going forward into the universe.
I think no more significant contribution has Apollo made to history.
It's not often that you can foretell history, but I think we can in this case.
And I think everybody ought to feel very proud of that fact.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: I'll see you in a little bit.
transcript.speaker: Fletcher

tokens: Let's go to the AL(SEP)...Okay, Bob.
I owe you a bias reading.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Or you can get it later.
And we're off to the ALSEP...
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: (Waving into the TV) I'm going to give it to you right now.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: I presume you've a UHT out at the ALSEP, Jack.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: Well, I love it, and I'm sure it did a good job...
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Well, we're not through with you, Gene, so don't throw yourself too far!
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: I just don't want to hit old Challenger there.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: I didn't throw it as far as I could have.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: ...we timed the parabola for that, and we have one excellent measurement of "g" on the Moon now.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: Yeah, I didn't get you a pendulum, but I don't know where I would, Bob.
I'm going to have to take you out to the VIP site!
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: We're ready for that, and we'll...
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: (Looking at his checklist) Well, let me make sure I got everything.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Bob, I guess you're reading me through the LM, huh?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: You guys both read me through the LM?
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: The first thing I want to do...Tell John (Young) I'm going to do it exactly like he wants.
(Pause) The (LMP) camera is under the seat, I hope.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Jack, did you do something with the
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: It was under the seat, right?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: I want to make sure it is because I'll need it out there.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: I don't know that it's there now.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Well, I want to make sure that I can get...that I've got something to dust with (so the Rover won't overheat).
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: (Joking, very dryly) And, Jack, as you go out to the ALSEP, let me cue in on your next 3 hours worth of work out there, repairing the ALSEP.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: Number one, we want to retrieve the UHT.

And I quote, "tap sharply" - that's "sharply" - on the (LSG) gimbal, which is the center section there, the little square metal piece in the middle...tap sharply on the gimbal with the UHT, and then reverify the level on the LSG.
We'll check response here in Mission Control after you've done that.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: (Incredulous) You mean tap on the thing (the bar) that swings!?!
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: You always wanted to do that, didn't you?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Well, let me see if I can have a clean UHT to do that.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Everything (that is, the Nav system) is zeroed.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: And I'll be talking to Jack here, Gene, for a while.
You can interrupt with your comments talking over me, and I'll try and copy them.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: I got a (warning) flag on the other battery (at) 139 degrees.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: It's probably not heavily, but sharply.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: You can see that little square metal piece in the middle there?
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: You see that little square metal piece on there?
You can just sort of rap on that...
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: You want me to do it again?
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: That was sort of a moderate "hard tap".
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Go ahead, and hit it harder.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: I can hit it harder yet.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Bob, you might be getting TV the way the antenna's oriented right now.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: We'll do some more stuff here.
In the meanwhile, while they're thinking about what's wrong with it...Did you just tap it again?
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: I'm over at the Central Station now.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Now, we want to take some photographs at the Central Station and a few selected photographs of the ALSEP.
Number one, we want a "7-foot cross-Sun" to the south of the ALSEP Central Station and then a 7-foot down-Sun of the Central Station.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: You might tell me what they're trying to get with it.
I might be able to help them.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: I presume that what this means is looking to the south.
I suspect that's what happened here, the way it was written up.
So it's a 7-foot looking at all the switches to make sure you guys turned them the right way, I suppose.
And then a 7-foot looking down-Sun, so that would be facing west, that side of it.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: Now, there's a problem with the Central Station in which they think the south end is buried more deeply in the dirt than they had intended.
And the Central Station is, at the present time, getting very warm on the back side, on the south side there.
They believe you probably buried it in the ground when you were trying to tilt it to the proper alignment.
They're requesting that, when you're at the ALSEP, you remove any soil buildup or debris with a convenient tool.
They don't want you to touch it because it's fairly warm.
But if you have a UHT or something to move it...Do you have a UHT with you or something with you that you can brush that soil aside with?
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: (Pause) And you can give me a call...
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: Fortunately, I brought my handy-dandy Rover sampler out.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: You can brush that (dirt) aside, and give me a call when you think that's cleared up the way it ought to be.
That's probably one of those things we didn't think about when we decided to tilt the Central Station.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: Well, you couldn't anticipate the soil, Bob.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: I thought I was, but I think I may move just a little bit.
There's a little rise here I can give you.
(Pause) I think I'll give it to you.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: By the way, Bob, the soil gets more cohesive with depth.
I hadn't really noticed that before.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: It's quite a bit more cohesive at...(It) feels about the same down to 3 centimeters out here; and then the cohesiveness goes up so (much that) it's difficult to scrape with the Rover sampler.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: (To Houston) Well, I think you can see almost everything from here.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: And, Jack, let me know when you get done scraping that soil away.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: And now comes the hardest (high-gain antenna) alignment of them all!
See if I can't tweak it up for you.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Bob, the east-west level bubble (on the Central Station) is not quite level (that is, it is not quite centered).
Do you want me to tweak that up?
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Yeah, you might tweak that up.
We are getting a good signal, but go ahead and tweak it up just a little bit.
(Pause) And, Gene, what are you doing these days?
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: I'm getting the high-gain set up for you.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Now you know why we didn't make you park it in that orientation all the time, don't you?
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: It was a piece of cake up until now.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Bob, you're looking right down the center of my eyepiece.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Yeah, we're getting TV there, Geno.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: Well, let me take a look, and clean things up.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: I guess you can dust and dust and dust some more for a while.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: Let me get this dusting problem out of the way before I do anything else.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: You can look at your vantage point, and if you don't like it, let me know.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: How close can soil be to this back plate of the ALSEP?
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: It's about 30 centimeters away, most of the places now.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: We'd like you to return to the surface gravimeter, Jack.
What you did had some effect, but not a lasting effect.
And we'd like you to "rap even more sharply", more strongly, on the gimbal another three times.
And we're again watching it, and we'll let you know what to do.

(Pause) And I might tell you that this has all been done recently - this afternoon - up at Bendix on the "qual(ification)" unit, and it survived it and so we aren't in any real danger, apparently, of destroying it.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: Bob, don't let me forget to bring a
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: I'll mark that down and remind you.
And, Jack, you'll be glad to know that the temperature of the back plate there (on the Central Station) has already dropped twenty degrees - two-zero (20) degrees.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: Bob, I don't think that (Central Station) bubble is working.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: But why don't you go to the surface gravimeter?
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: ...Get out of the way please, Jack (means Gene); and we'll take a look against some bright soil.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: No, that's me he's talking to.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: I don't have one of the
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: It looks pretty good to us.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: It's really fighting us pretty hard.
We'd like you to put the UHT in the socket, and rock it very firmly.
Don't pick it up, but rock it very firmly from side to side in all four directions.
Move the UHT about 6 inches in each direction while you're doing it.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: And the level bubble is (pause) centered.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: We understand it's in good configuration again as far as alignment and leveling is concerned, Jack.
Let's go on and take some more ALSEP photos, and let them think about it for a minute.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: Next, what we want is some heat flow (photos)...(Pause) Okay.
They'd like to do it one more time, and then call it quits.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: The rocking bit one more time.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: Bob, I may have moved the high-gain.
Do you see any change in signal?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: If you're happy, I won't touch it.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: The shade is aligned to the Sun now; and it's level.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: We copy that, and let's go get some ALSEP photos, Jack.
I think you got some heat flow photos the other night, besides the two pans.
If you did, these may be redundant.
They want the cross-Sun and down-Sun of the east hole and cross-Sun and down-Sun of the west hole.
And I'm not sure but what you got those earlier.
You said you got some extra heat flow, but tell me if you did.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: I'll get the heat flow pictures.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: I think all they're asking for is the two 7-foot stereopairs.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: What they're asking for, Jack, is a 7-foot down-Sun and a 7-foot cross-Sun, which isn't quite what we've been taking in the past.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: I'm getting the standard ones, Bob.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: They can't complain about that, certainly.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: We'd like a 3-foot shot of the lunar mass spectrometer (LMS), including the orifice where the breakshield was.
And, Geno, we are observing some degradation and would like to have the high-gain...
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: We'd like to get the high-gain re-oriented a little bit.
We're observing some degradation in the picture.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: Now we want to go over (to) the neutron flux, Jack.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: The panel you want covered...(To himself) Yeah, that's the panel.
(To Bob) You want the panel with the On/Off switch and the signal strength switch and so forth covered, don't you?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: And be sure to get the thing to External before you cover it there, Gene.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: That was going to be a question of mine.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: What do you want me to do at the neutron flux?
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: We want a photograph facing south from 7-foot.
So a 7-foot cross-Sun, essentially, of the neutron flux in the soil.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: (Pause) Would you like to have the RTG in that picture?
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Oh, I suppose if you're generous, you might take a partial pan around to the RTG.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: Well, it's just about that direction.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Now let's remove...(repeating for emphasis) remove the neutron probe experiment from the ground, and turn it off.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: No more on the gravimeter, huh?
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: No, the gravimeter is looking very bad, still.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: Bob, do you want...(Stops to listen)
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: And, Jack, you might note as you withdraw (the neutron probe)...
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: ...just how difficult it is to withdraw it.
Whether or not it's been seized by the soil collapsing around it or not.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: Not at all, not at all.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: It won't be, I'll tell you!
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: And I'm giving the LCRU another zap here (with the
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Is that upper or lower, Jack?
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: Aux Power circuit breaker is On.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: And the lower probe is capped.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Okay; and, Gene, you need to close that (Rover) Caution and Warning flag.
It's a heat sink when it's open, I guess.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: You want me to put a bag in front of that thing?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Want me to put a bag in front of it in case it pops open again?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: I can't imagine why it's really a problem anyway, because we got the Bypass On, there, and that heat's not going anywhere.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: Okay; and all the switches off.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Bob, you want me away from the ALSEP now?
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: I'll get one more word before we come back to the LM.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: (To himself) Okay, I'll get a camera.
I'm going to look under the seats one more time.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Jack, we're ready to leave the ALSEP.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: (Ironic) Well, I hate to do that, Bob.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: I'm sorry about that gravimeter, though.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Well, you're not the only one.
(Pause) The word is down here there's a whole room full of people who are sorry.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: Nothing in here (under the seats) but couple of old bags.
We used about all the bags we had, Jack!
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Okay; let me get one parting shot (photo) one of the finest running little machines I've ever had the pleasure to drive.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: And, Geno, some people down here are concerned about whether you've opened the battery covers or not.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: Oh, what a nice little machine!
Parked on a little downslope, but at the heading you want, and I guess Ed's satisfied with the TV response, huh?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: We're satisfied with the TV, Gene.
We're ready for you to take the EP number 3.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: Good old Mother Earth is right smack in the center (of the bore site on the high-gain antenna).
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Bob, while we've got a quiet moment here, as I go to deploy that EP charge, I'd just like to say that any part of Apollo 17 - or any part of Apollo - that has been a success thus far is probably, for the most part, due to the thousands of people in the aerospace industry who have given a great deal - besides dedication and besides effort and besides professionalism - to make it all a reality.
And I would just like to thank them.

Because what we've done here and what has been done in the past - as a matter of fact, what has been done for 200 years - you've got to contribute (means "attribute") to the spirit of the group of people who form the aerospace industry.
And I (say) "God bless you" and "thank you."
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Well, we're just two little sets of twinkletoes here.
There's a lot that goes to getting this Rover running out here that we don't have much to do with.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: And I guess there might be someone else that has something to do with it too, and I've been reading His sign - maybe not from Him directly, but His in spirit - as we run up and down that ladder.
And that's "Godspeed, the crew of Apollo 17".
And if He's listening, I'd like to thank Him, too.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: I'm at the end of the west SEP antenna.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Pin 3 is pulled, and it's still safe.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Wonder what I would do if it wasn't.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: And now, also, do you have the SEP transmitter turned off there, Gene?
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: Then we're ready for you guys...
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: ...to get back to the LM and dust and get in.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: ...it (the charge)'s setting right adjacent to the ring (the SEP antenna spool) on the west end, and I'm going to go back and turn the SEP off.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: And when that's done, Gene, we're ready for you and your
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: Great as an experience as it has been, I'd say we're probably both ready.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Hey, Bob, 55 Yankee is an exotic-looking rock I found about 5 meters south of the neutron flux hole.
It's just that there aren't many of them around here, and so I picked it up.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Jack, you'll always be picking rocks.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: There's so many interesting things around here.
(Pause) (To himself) Just don't lose your brush, Gene.
Okay, Bob, according to my inventory, I'm going to return to the LM and the (LMP's) camera's going to the ETB.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: (Laughs) We'll need a bias reading if you want to use it again, Gene.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: Come to think of it, I guess you are (done with the
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: I need a "locator" here to the LM.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Okay; and, 17, we need you guys in the LM in one-five minutes - fifteen minutes - because of oxygen constraints.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: Okay, Bob, my pictures are taken; I'm on the way.
Where else can you do this?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: (At the MESA) If I had landed 30 meters back, Jack, we'd be pitched down 5 degrees.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Okay, what they're saying is, I don't need my hammer any more.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: All we've got to dust and get in.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: ...We want you to dust and get in.
We got one-four (14) minutes remaining before we need the hatch closed.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: I don't know where I am.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: You ready to go on up?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Got to take my camera off.
I got another batch of pictures...the LM and the flag and...
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Let me have your camera (to put in the ETB).
(Noticing that Gene is about to throw the hammer) Oh, the poor little...Let me throw the hammer.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: You ought to be able to throw it.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Looked like it was going a million miles, but it really didn't.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Okay, here, this is an ETB.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Let me make sure that that's all cinched up.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: We got to get going here.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Unfortunately, their little plan didn't count for the fact that it's hard to pack the ETB with the film magazines in it.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: And I'll try and get the big bag here cinched up.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Yes, that's going to be...Oh, is it heavy.
Something in that core tube you put in there?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Don't tell anybody, though, because they'll get mad at me.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Oh, man, is that (big bag) heavy.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: (Garbled) 252 has about three-quarters of a core - hand pushed (into the ground) - (taken about) half a meter inside the
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Okay, Jack, how's that ETB coming so you can get going?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: I've got to put it on the strap, though.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Why don't you start on up?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Set that down, and I'll hand it all to you.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Okay, you'll have to hand stuff...
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: We need you inside in 10 minutes.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: (To Jack) I'll get your front real quick.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: I'll do a lot of jumping up here in a minute.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: I'll get the legs as best I can.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Why don't you get me here before you do that?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: And while I'm doing that, will you take this...
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: I think we're just going to have to live with it.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: I can kick a lot of that stuff off my legs.
(Pause) How do I look in back?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: (Pause) You're going to have to...Your legs are really filthy.
Not much I can do about it.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Just kick them against each other when you go up.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: And don't forget your PLSS an...
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: Although it doesn't make much difference anymore.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: That's to get them out of the way.
The big bag didn't stay closed very long.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: I don't know how we're going to get that in.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Well, I can hand it to you.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: It never had a very good closure on it...
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: I had it over the top.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Oh, it won't hold with all that weight in there.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Okay, let me get a high...Got to close this.
You don't want that in your way.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Will that bother you getting in?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Okay, why don't you hand me the neutron flux, and I'll put it...
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Why don't you start in, and I'll get some of these bags (the full SCBs) out of the way.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Well, I just...(Pause) You don't want to hand them to me up here?
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: And, Jack, for your thoughts, we've agreed that you can delete the tracking light.
We'd like to get you guys in as soon as possible.
And we'll delete the tracking light test.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: Okay, that's all I can handle up here.
One more and I can put it up here.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Watch the cover on this one.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Stand it up, because the cover won't hack it.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Soon as you get on in, I'll come up to the porch.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Where's that EVA pallet that's always in my way?
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: I think we aren't going to have one of those (an EVA) tomorrow, Jack, so we did away with that.
(Pause) We hope we're not going to have one of those tomorrow.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: Bob, we're maximizing our efforts, so just bear with us.
Jack's going to be in about 30 seconds, and I'm on the ladder hauling some stuff up now.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: I just don't want you to.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: (In the cabin by now) Got some stuff for me?
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: (Pause) This is not the time to rush.
It's the time to do it nice and slow and right.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: (Pause) You're not going to like this (big bag), but I'm going to give you this one first because I've got it in my hand.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Oh, hang in there...I've got it!
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Let me tell you, that's heavy.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: If not, I'll shove it in further.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Big bag, three SRCs (means "SCBs") and a neutron flux.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Okay, and we gather an ETB coming up with two cameras in it.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: And as you guys say farewell to the Moon, we're looking up to the Earth (with the Rover TV camera) down here where you guys are returning pretty soon.
transcript.speaker: Parker

tokens: You're going to have to push that.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Okay, let me get that other thing (garbled).
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Bob, this is Gene, and I'm on the surface; and, as I take man's last step from the surface, back home for some time to come - but we believe not too long into the future - I'd like to just (say) what I believe history will record.
That America's challenge of today has forged man's destiny of tomorrow.
And, as we leave the Moon at Taurus- Littrow, we leave as we came and, God willing, as we shall return, with peace and hope for all mankind.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Bob, I am up on the ladder and I'm going to be going through the hatch.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Gene, I've got to get out of your way.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Okay, let me...(Pause) Okay, babe, here I come.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Let me look at that hatch once more.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: That's the last time we want to have to open that.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Caught in the same way again (on the DSKY).
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Let me just...I can see down there.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Does it look good to you?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: There is a little bit of dust but it's all in the...I don't think the seal's affected.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Yes, I've got to get out of your way.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt

tokens: Let me get my hand over here.
Okay, I'm out of your way.
transcript.speaker: Schmitt
